past_medical_history: Iron deficiency anemia, diagnosed 2 years ago, resolved with supplementation
Mild intermittent asthma since childhood, well-controlled
Urinary tract infection 8 months ago, treated with nitrofurantoin
family_history: No known family history of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 2-3 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in a second-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Previously ran 3-4 times per week; has reduced activity over the past month due to leg heaviness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent, coherent, and without dysarthria. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Recall 3/3 at registration and 3/3 at five minutes. No evidence of cognitive slowing or inattention. Mood appears mildly anxious; affect is appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Visual acuity 20/20 OD, 20/25 OS. Mild relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) noted in the left eye on swinging flashlight test. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam reveals mild temporal pallor of the left optic disc. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone mildly increased in bilateral lower extremities with a spastic quality, more pronounced on the right. Upper extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally. Lower extremity strength: hip flexion 4+/5 bilaterally, knee extension 4/5 right and 4+/5 left, ankle dorsiflexion 4+/5 right and 5/5 left. No pronator drift in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout. Pinprick sensation diminished below the level of the umbilicus bilaterally (approximately T10 distribution). Vibration sense reduced at the right great toe (5 seconds compared to 12 seconds at the left great toe). Proprioception intact in upper extremities, mildly impaired at the right great toe. Romberg sign positive with sway to the right.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Upper extremities: 2+ biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. Lower extremities: 3+ at patellar bilaterally, 3+ at Achilles bilaterally. Plantar response extensor (Babinski sign) on the right, equivocal on the left. Hoffman sign negative bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact without dysmetria bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing reveals mild imprecision on the right. Rapid alternating movements intact in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic gait with circumduction of the right leg. Tandem gait impaired with two missteps over ten steps. Unable to perform heel walking on the right without difficulty. Toe walking preserved bilaterally.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive — patient reports electric shock-like sensation radiating down the spine with neck flexion. No Uhthoff phenomenon reported currently, though patient notes that her leg weakness seemed worse after a hot shower last week.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive bilateral leg weakness and urinary urgency over the past three weeks
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Thompson is a 28-year-old right-handed graphic designer who presents to the neurology service after being referred from urgent care for progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness and new-onset urinary urgency. Her symptoms began approximately three weeks ago with a sensation of heaviness in both legs that she initially attributed to overexertion from a weekend hiking trip. Over the following days, the heaviness progressed to noticeable weakness, particularly in the right leg, making it difficult for her to climb the stairs to her second-floor apartment without gripping the railing tightly. She also reports a band-like tightness around her lower abdomen that has been constant since about two weeks ago, which she describes as feeling like a belt cinched too tightly around her waist.

Approximately ten days ago, she developed urinary urgency, noting that she frequently feels the need to urinate with little warning and has had two episodes of near-incontinence. She denies frank urinary retention or bowel dysfunction. She also reports intermittent tingling and numbness in both feet, worse on the right, that has been gradually worsening over the past two weeks.

When questioned about prior neurological symptoms, Ms. Thompson recalls an episode approximately eighteen months ago where she experienced blurred vision in her left eye lasting about two weeks. She describes the vision as being like looking through a frosted window and notes that it was accompanied by mild pain behind the eye, particularly with eye movement. She was seen by an ophthalmologist who diagnosed possible retrobulbar optic neuritis and recommended follow-up, but she did not pursue further workup at the time as her vision improved spontaneously. She denies any other prior neurological symptoms, including seizures, limb weakness, speech difficulties, or swallowing problems. She has not had any recent infections, fevers, or vaccinations. Her general health has been otherwise good aside from the conditions listed in her past medical history.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, predominantly along the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Largest lesion measures 12 x 8 mm in the right periventricular region
findings.borders: Ovoid, oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricles in some lesions

findings.location: Right centrum semiovale, subcortical white matter
Left juxtacortical white matter, posterior frontal lobe
Dorsal aspect of the cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level
findings.mass_effect: Mild cord swelling at C3 level

additional_observations: Total of 7 white matter lesions identified across periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord regions
Several periventricular lesions demonstrate ovoid morphology oriented perpendicular to the ventricles, though classic Dawson finger morphology is not definitively seen
No infratentorial lesions identified on this study
Ventricles and sulci are normal in size and configuration for patient age
mri.impression: Multiple white matter lesions in periventricular and juxtacortical distribution with a single enhancing lesion in the right centrum semiovale, and an additional lesion in the dorsal cervical cord at C3-C4 with faint enhancement. The distribution and morphology of lesions are suspicious for a demyelinating process. The presence of at least one enhancing and multiple non-enhancing lesions suggests dissemination in time. Clinical correlation is recommended. Differential considerations include demyelinating disease (most likely), though CNS vasculitis and neurosarcoidosis should be considered.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low — lesions are not longitudinally extensive in the cord
Low — pattern more typical for demyelinating disease

recommended_actions: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
Consider MRI thoracic spine to assess for additional spinal cord lesions
Clinical correlation with visual evoked potentials

labs.interpretation: All routine blood work within normal limits. CBC is normal with no evidence of active infection or anemia. BMP is normal. Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) are not elevated. ANA is negative. Vitamin B12 and TSH are normal. ACE level is normal, making neurosarcoidosis less likely. These results are nonspecific and do not point toward a particular etiology but are consistent with a demyelinating process where systemic labs are typically normal.

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Present — 3 bands unique to CSF, not found in serum (paired serum/CSF analysis)

csf.interpretation: CSF analysis reveals mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (8 cells/uL) and mildly elevated protein (52 mg/dL) with normal glucose. Oligoclonal bands are present (3 bands unique to CSF) and the IgG index is borderline elevated at 0.68. Myelin basic protein is mildly elevated, suggesting active demyelination. These findings are consistent with an intrathecal inflammatory process and are supportive of, though not diagnostic for, multiple sclerosis. The borderline nature of the IgG index and relatively few oligoclonal bands create some diagnostic uncertainty.

Visual Evoked Potentials.clinical_significance: Prolonged P100 latency in the left eye is consistent with prior optic neuritis and demyelination of the left optic nerve. This finding supports dissemination in space for MS diagnosis.
Normal P100 latency in the right eye.
Low-normal amplitude in the left eye.
Significant interocular latency asymmetry supports unilateral optic nerve demyelination on the left.

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials demonstrate prolonged P100 latency in the left eye (118 ms) with a significant interocular latency difference of 21 ms. These findings are consistent with demyelination of the left optic nerve, likely related to the patient's prior episode of optic neuritis. The right eye responses are normal. This provides electrophysiological evidence of a lesion disseminated in space from the spinal cord and brain lesions.
abnormal_values_summary: P100 latency — left eye: 118 ms ms - Prolonged P100 latency in the left eye is consistent with prior optic neuritis and demyelination of the left optic nerve. This finding supports dissemination in space for MS diagnosis.
Interocular latency difference: 21 ms ms - Significant interocular latency asymmetry supports unilateral optic nerve demyelination on the left.

signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Faint patchy enhancement at the superior margin

findings.mass_effect: Mild focal cord swelling at C3

additional_observations: Both spinal cord lesions are short-segment (less than 2 vertebral segments each), peripherally located, and involve less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord
No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis pattern (argues against NMOSD)
Conus medullaris and cauda equina are normal in appearance
No vertebral body or disc abnormalities
output.impression: Two short-segment spinal cord lesions identified in the dorsal cervical cord (C3-C4, with faint enhancement) and dorsal thoracic cord (T6-T7, without enhancement). The lesion characteristics — short-segment, peripheral, partial cross-section involvement — are typical of multiple sclerosis. The thoracic lesion at T6-T7 likely correlates with the patient's sensory level at approximately T10 and lower extremity symptoms. No features suggestive of NMOSD (no longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis).

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Very low — lesions are short segment

recommended_actions: Correlate with brain MRI and CSF findings
Consider aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD

OCT — Retinal Nerve Fiber Layer.clinical_significance: Thinning of the RNFL in the left eye is consistent with prior optic neuritis and axonal loss in the left optic nerve.
Temporal RNFL thinning is the most characteristic OCT finding following optic neuritis.
Ganglion cell layer thinning supports retrograde axonal degeneration from prior optic neuritis.

output.interpretation: OCT demonstrates reduced RNFL thickness in the left eye (average 72 um) with particular thinning in the temporal quadrant (48 um) and ganglion cell layer thinning (68 um). The right eye values are within normal limits. These findings are consistent with prior optic neuritis in the left eye with resultant axonal loss. The temporal predominance of RNFL thinning is characteristic of the papillomacular bundle damage typically seen following optic neuritis in multiple sclerosis.
abnormal_values_summary: RNFL thickness — left eye (average): 72 um um - Thinning of the RNFL in the left eye is consistent with prior optic neuritis and axonal loss in the left optic nerve.
RNFL temporal quadrant — left eye: 48 um um - Temporal RNFL thinning is the most characteristic OCT finding following optic neuritis.
Ganglion cell layer — left eye: 68 um um - Ganglion cell layer thinning supports retrograde axonal degeneration from prior optic neuritis.

Demyelinating Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Negative AQP4 antibody makes NMOSD very unlikely.
Negative MOG antibody makes MOGAD unlikely.

output.interpretation: Both aquaporin-4 (AQP4) and MOG antibodies are negative. These results effectively exclude neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) and MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) as diagnoses. In the setting of the patient's clinical presentation and MRI findings, these negative results further support a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.

findings.size: Stable, largest 12 x 8 mm right periventricular

signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No enhancement (previously enhancing right centrum semiovale lesion no longer enhances)

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, posterior horn of lateral ventricle
Left juxtacortical white matter, posterior frontal lobe

additional_observations: Previously enhancing lesion in right centrum semiovale no longer enhances, consistent with resolved active inflammation
One new enhancing lesion identified near the posterior horn of the left lateral ventricle
Total lesion burden is mildly increased compared to prior study
output.impression: Interval development of one new enhancing periventricular lesion near the left posterior lateral ventricle with resolution of enhancement in the previously active right centrum semiovale lesion. These findings confirm ongoing subclinical disease activity and further support dissemination in time. The overall pattern is consistent with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis with active disease despite clinical stability.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis with ongoing activity

recommended_actions: Initiation or escalation of disease-modifying therapy given evidence of ongoing disease activity
Continue routine MRI surveillance every 6-12 months

contraindications: Absolute lymphocyte count must be monitored; discontinue if sustained below 500 cells/uL
warnings: May cause flushing and GI side effects, especially in the first month
Rare risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) with prolonged lymphopenia
Monitor CBC with differential every 6 months
output.formulary_status: Tier 3 specialty medication, prior authorization required
alternatives: Glatiramer acetate 40 mg subcutaneous three times weekly (injectable, lower efficacy tier)
Ocrelizumab 600 mg IV every 6 months (higher efficacy, consider if high disease activity)

output.query: multiple sclerosis relapsing-remitting first-line disease modifying therapy 2024 guidelines

results.title: Practice guideline recommendations summary: Disease-modifying therapies for adults with multiple sclerosis
results.key_finding: Clinicians should offer DMTs to patients with relapsing forms of MS with recent clinical relapses or MRI activity. Starting therapy early is associated with better long-term outcomes.

results.title: 2021 MAGNIMS-CMSC-NAIMS consensus recommendations on the use of MRI in MS
results.key_finding: Reference MRI within 6 months of diagnosis, follow-up MRI at 6-12 months to assess treatment efficacy. New T2 or enhancing lesions on follow-up MRI constitute evidence of disease activity.

results.title: McDonald criteria revisions for MS diagnosis
results.key_finding: Oligoclonal bands in CSF can substitute for dissemination in time, allowing earlier diagnosis. Simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions satisfy DIT on a single MRI.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend early initiation of disease-modifying therapy in patients with relapsing-remitting MS. First-line options include moderate-efficacy therapies (dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide, glatiramer acetate, interferons) or higher-efficacy therapies (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab) depending on disease activity and prognostic factors. The 2017 McDonald criteria allow CSF oligoclonal bands to contribute to dissemination in time, enabling earlier diagnosis and treatment initiation.

differential.key_features: Spinal cord lesions are short-segment rather than longitudinally extensive; AQP4 antibodies negative; brain lesion distribution typical for MS rather than NMOSD
MOG antibodies negative; prior optic neuritis episode had good recovery but without the prominent disc edema typically seen in MOGAD
Normal inflammatory markers; lesion distribution and morphology more consistent with demyelination; no systemic vasculitis features
Normal ACE level; no leptomeningeal enhancement; no pulmonary or systemic findings; lesion pattern not typical for sarcoidosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history focusing on prior neurological episodes, symptom timeline, and progression pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History of prior optic neuritis episode 18 months ago with spontaneous recovery, suggesting dissemination in time
Upper motor neuron signs in lower extremities (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign), sensory level at T10, RAPD in left eye, positive Lhermitte sign

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain and cervical spine with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple periventricular and juxtacortical white matter lesions with one enhancing lesion; cervical cord lesion at C3-C4

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present (3 bands unique to CSF), borderline elevated IgG index, mild pleocytosis, elevated myelin basic protein

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup (CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, TSH) to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All routine labs within normal limits, excluding common MS mimics

optimal_actions.action: Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both antibodies negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to evaluate for subclinical optic nerve demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in left eye confirming prior optic nerve demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Apply McDonald 2017 diagnostic criteria to confirm MS diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Criteria met for dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord lesions) and dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, plus CSF oligoclonal bands)

optimal_actions.action: Administer IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3-5 days for acute relapse management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in acute symptoms of myelopathy over days to weeks

optimal_actions.action: Initiate disease-modifying therapy after discussion of options with patient
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selection of appropriate DMT based on disease activity, patient preferences, and safety profile

optimal_actions.action: Refer to MS specialist for ongoing disease management and arrange follow-up MRI in 3-6 months
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Establishment of long-term care plan with monitoring protocol

critical_actions: Obtain MRI brain and spinal cord with gadolinium to assess for demyelinating lesions
Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands
Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Apply McDonald 2017 criteria to establish MS diagnosis
Treat acute relapse with IV corticosteroids
Initiate disease-modifying therapy to prevent future relapses and disability progression
contraindicated_actions: Starting chronic opioid therapy for neuropathic pain without appropriate neuropathic pain agents
Performing brain biopsy without exhausting less invasive diagnostic workup
Administering live vaccines while on immunosuppressive disease-modifying therapy
Prescribing interferon-beta without checking for pre-existing depression or hepatic dysfunction
Delaying disease-modifying therapy initiation without clinical justification
key_reasoning_points: The combination of subacute myelopathy with prior optic neuritis episode suggests a relapsing CNS inflammatory process with dissemination in space and time
MRI findings of periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord lesions with both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions are highly suggestive of MS
The borderline CSF findings (only 3 oligoclonal bands, IgG index of 0.68) create moderate diagnostic uncertainty appropriate for this difficulty level
Absence of Dawson fingers on imaging and no infratentorial lesions make this a less classic presentation than straightforward cases
Short-segment spinal cord lesions and negative AQP4/MOG antibodies argue strongly against NMOSD and MOGAD
Lhermitte sign and Uhthoff phenomenon are characteristic clinical features that support the diagnosis of MS
The patient's age, sex, and ethnicity fit the typical demographic profile for MS
